critical_issues.title: SSH credentials stored in plaintext
critical_issues.description: SSH credentials (username, password) from POST /api/switches/<id>/collect are queued for collection without encryption. If these are stored in database or raw_outputs, they will be in plaintext.
evidence: app.py L53: collector.collect_switch(db_path, switch_id, username, password)
No encryption applied before DB storage or file write
critical_issues.impact: Compromise of network device credentials; lateral movement; network reconnaissance

steps: 1. Hash credentials using PBKDF2-SHA256 before storage (non-reversible for comparison)
2. Alternatively: use OS-level secrets storage (Windows Credential Manager / DPAPI)
3. OR: use 3rd-party Secrets Manager (HashiCorp Vault, LastPass API)
4. Update core/collector.py to retrieve credentials from secure storage

critical_issues.cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
critical_issues.title: No API authentication required
critical_issues.description: All API endpoints (/api/state, /api/switches/<id>/collect, /api/switches/<id>/detail) are accessible without authentication. Anyone on localhost can trigger switch collections or access network topology.
evidence: app.py L25: @app.route('/api/state', methods=['GET']) - no auth decorator
No middleware checking API tokens or session cookies
critical_issues.impact: Unauthorized access to collected network topology; arbitrary collection triggers; DoS by queue saturation

steps: 1. Add Bearer token authentication via Authorization header (RFC 6750)
3. Store API tokens hashed in database (PBKDF2-SHA256)

critical_issues.cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
critical_issues.title: Sensitive information in error logs
critical_issues.description: SSH errors from netmiko (line 151) are logged as-is via logger.error(). These can include partial credentials, device responses, or stack traces containing sensitive paths.
evidence: Unfiltered exception messages may leak connection strings, credential hints, or internal IPs
critical_issues.impact: Credential hints or internal network details in log files; compliance violation (PCI-DSS 3.4, SOC 2)

steps: 3. Log error type only, not full exception message

high_issues.cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code
high_issues.title: No input validation on JSON request bodies
high_issues.description: request.get_json() is called without schema validation. Malformed JSON or unexpected fields could trigger unhandled exceptions or undefined behavior.
evidence: app.py L47-48: Direct .get() on untrusted data
high_issues.impact: HTTP 500 errors exposing stack traces; potential DoS via large payloads; type confusion attacks

steps: 1. Define JSON schema using pydantic BaseModel
2. Example: class CollectRequest(BaseModel): switch_id: int; username: str; password: str
4. Catch ValidationError and return 400 with sanitized message

high_issues.title: Debug mode can be enabled via environment variable in production
high_issues.description: config.py L58-59 allows DEBUG environment variable to override config, enabling Flask debug mode. Debug mode exposes stack traces, request data, and interactive debugger access.
high_issues.impact: Information disclosure (stack traces, source code); interactive debugger access; RCE if debugger PIN is guessable

steps: 3. Set debug=False unconditionally in production (use FLASK_ENV=production)
4. If needed, use FLASK_ENV (standard Flask practice) instead
5. Add CI check: ensure debug=False in release builds

high_issues.cwe: CWE-770: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
high_issues.title: No rate limiting on API endpoints
high_issues.description: POST /api/switches/<id>/collect can be called repeatedly without rate limiting. Queue size is 100 (core/collector.py L25), but no per-IP rate limit prevents abuse.
high_issues.impact: Queue saturation DoS; resource exhaustion; CPU spike from SSH connection attempts

steps: 2. Add @limiter.limit('5 per minute') decorator to POST /api/switches/<id>/collect
3. Add @limiter.limit('30 per minute') decorator to GET endpoints
4. Store limits in config.yaml (configurable per environment)

high_issues.cwe: CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
high_issues.title: No validation of SSH target IP address
high_issues.description: SSH connection is made to switch['ip'] (from database) without validation. No check for private IP ranges, localhost, or restricted networks prevents potential SSRF-like abuse.
high_issues.impact: SSRF to internal services; access to RFC 1918 networks; access to localhost services

medium_issues.cwe: CWE-327: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
medium_issues.title: SQLite database file has no encryption
medium_issues.description: SQLite database (netdash.db) is stored in plaintext. Contains switch credentials, network topology, and MAC/ARP tables. No filesystem encryption or database-level encryption applied.
evidence: No cipher/encryption flag on SQLite connection
Stored in project root, accessible to any process with file permissions
medium_issues.impact: Disk access compromise exposes credentials, network topology, host inventory

steps: 1. Use SQLCipher (sqlite3 with AES-256 encryption): pip install sqlcipher3
2. OR use OS-level encryption (BitLocker on Windows, FileVault on macOS)
3. OR store DB in encrypted VM volume
5. Note: Deferred to M4~M5 per PRD (M6 reserved for DPAPI)

medium_issues.title: No pinned dependency versions in requirements.txt
medium_issues.description: Requirements file does not pin exact versions. This allows installation of vulnerable packages if latest versions have known CVEs.
evidence: requirements.txt likely contains: flask, netmiko, pyyaml (without version pins)
medium_issues.impact: Transitive dependency vulnerabilities; supply chain compromise; CI/CD supply chain risk

steps: 1. Pin all direct dependencies: flask==3.0.0, netmiko==4.3.0, pyyaml==6.0.1
2. Generate lock file: pip freeze > requirements-lock.txt
3. Use pip-audit to scan for CVEs: pip install pip-audit && pip-audit
5. Add CI check: pip audit on every build

medium_issues.cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
medium_issues.title: No environment separation (dev/staging/prod)
medium_issues.description: Single config.yaml for all environments. Secrets and environment-specific settings are not separated. Risk of deploying dev settings to production.
evidence: config.py L26-33: All settings loaded into single Config object
medium_issues.impact: Dev/staging credentials exposed in production; debug features enabled in prod; compliance violation

steps: 4. config.py: load config/[APP_ENV].yaml based on APP_ENV

low_issues.title: No CORS headers or Content-Security-Policy
low_issues.description: Flask app does not set HTTP security headers (CORS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, etc.). Web UI could be vulnerable to framing attacks.
evidence: app.py: No response.headers.update() for security headers
No Flask-CORS or manual CORS setup
low_issues.impact: Clickjacking (if web UI added); potential information disclosure from error pages

steps: 3. Add security headers in after_request: response.headers['X-Frame-Options'] = 'DENY'

architectural_recommendations.title: Implement secure credential storage layer
architectural_recommendations.description: Create dedicated credentials.py module with encrypt/decrypt/hash methods. All credential operations flow through this layer.

architectural_recommendations.title: Add API authentication middleware
architectural_recommendations.description: Create auth.py with @require_api_token decorator. Protect all endpoints with this decorator.

architectural_recommendations.title: Implement input validation layer
architectural_recommendations.description: Create validators.py with pydantic models for all request bodies (CollectRequest, ConfigUpdate, etc.).

architectural_recommendations.title: Add error logging sanitizer
architectural_recommendations.description: Create logging.py with SanitizedFormatter to strip credentials, IPs, and sensitive paths from log messages.

compliance_notes: PCI-DSS 3.4: Sensitive authentication data must not be logged. Current logging fails this.
SOC 2: Requires encrypted credentials in storage and transmission. DPAPI deferral to M6 is a gap.
ISO 27001: Requires authentication controls on all sensitive data access. API lacks auth.
NIST SP 800-53 IA-2: Requires multi-factor authentication or strong single-factor for critical functions.

summary.verdict: Security review Round 3 identifies 2 CRITICAL vulnerabilities that must be fixed before M2-1 starts. Credential storage and API authentication are minimal requirements for any networked service. Additional HIGH/MEDIUM issues should be addressed within Phase 2~5 per implementation plan.
